summary: Ronacher argues that removing `str.encode`/`str.decode` in Python 3 was a misguided response to confusion that actually stemmed from Python 2’s default encoding behavior. He explains that the codec APIs are general-purpose and not limited to strings, and that legitimate string-to-string encodings (like base64) exist. The real confusion comes from the implicit ASCII decode step triggered by the default encoding when bytes are passed where Unicode is expected, producing misleading errors. He shows how disabling the default encoding makes the behavior explicit and the errors clearer. His conclusion is that the implicit ASCII default was the root cause, and Python 3’s removal of these methods unnecessarily harmed codec support.
key_points: The core confusion is Python 2’s implicit default encoding, not the existence of `str.encode`/`str.decode`.
Codecs in Python are object-based and allow valid string-to-string transformations (e.g., base64), so removing methods breaks real use cases.
Misleading errors arise because bytes get implicitly decoded with the default ASCII codec before encoding proceeds.
If implicit ASCII decoding had never existed, the Python 2→3 transition could have been simpler and more explicit.
Python 3’s removal of byte-to-byte and Unicode-to-Unicode codecs reduced functionality.
money_quotes: And all in all I believe that's a really sad example of where wrong conclusions were drawn.
The confusing part is not the codecs API but the Python default encoding.
Not only is that error message misleading, it also does not show up at all if the string does indeed fit into ascii.
There it does foo (bytes) -> ascii decode -> foo (Unicode) -> utf-8 encode -> foo (bytes).
If we would have just never started doing the implicit ASCII codec we would have solved so much confusion early on and everything would have been more explicit.
And we would not now end up with inferior codec support in Python 3 because the byte to byte and Unicode to Unicode codecs were removed.
key_insight: The real source of Python’s codec confusion was implicit default ASCII decoding, so removing `str.encode`/`str.decode` in Python 3 fixed the wrong problem and reduced functionality.
